title: Accessibility (A11y) Specialist Expert Assessment
description: Professional-level evaluation for Accessibility Specialists focusing on WCAG 2.2 standards, ARIA patterns, assistive technology compatibility, and inclusive design audits.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between WCAG 2.1 and WCAG 2.2 regarding accessibility requirements?
options: 2.2 removes all requirements for mobile devices
2.2 introduces nine new success criteria, including Focus Appearance and Redundant Entry
2.2 is only applicable to government websites
There are no functional differences between the two versions
questions.explanation: WCAG 2.2 builds on 2.1 by adding criteria to further help users with cognitive and learning disabilities, as well as users with low vision on mobile devices.

questions.text: When using `aria-hidden='true'`, what happens to the element in the browser?
options: It is hidden visually from the screen
It is removed from the Accessibility Tree but remains visible on the screen
It is disabled and cannot be clicked
It is announced as 'Secret' by screen readers
questions.explanation: aria-hidden only affects assistive technologies. It should never be used on focusable elements that are still visible.

questions.text: What is the minimum contrast ratio required for normal text (under 18pt) to meet WCAG AA standards?
questions.explanation: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.

questions.text: Which attribute should be used to provide a label for an element when the label text is visible on the screen?
options: aria-label
aria-labelledby
title
alt
questions.explanation: aria-labelledby is preferred when the label exists in the DOM, as it creates a direct relationship with the existing text node.

questions.text: What does the 'Meaningful Sequence' success criterion ensure?
options: That code is written in alphabetical order
That the reading order perceived by assistive technology matches the visual logical flow
That all videos have captions
That navigation links are always at the top
questions.explanation: If the order in which content is presented affects its meaning, the sequence must be programmatically determinable.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a 'Skip Link'?
options: To skip a song in a media player
To allow keyboard users to bypass repetitive content like headers and navigation to reach the main content
To bypass paywalls
To skip form validation
questions.explanation: Skip links are a WCAG requirement for pages with repeated blocks of content.

questions.text: When is an image considered 'Decorative', requiring a null alt attribute (alt='')?
options: When it is a photo of a person
When it provides no unique information and is used purely for visual styling or branding
When the image is very small
Images are never decorative
questions.explanation: Decorative images should be hidden from screen readers using a null alt attribute so they don't clutter the audio experience.

questions.text: What is the risk of using 'Positive Tabindex' (e.g., tabindex='1')?
options: It breaks the screen reader
It creates a confusing and unpredictable tab order that doesn't follow the DOM structure
It makes the element unclickable
It slows down page performance
questions.explanation: Tabindex should generally only be 0 (natural order) or -1 (programmatic focus only).

questions.text: Which ARIA live region value should be used for a critical error message that needs to interrupt the screen reader immediately?
options: aria-live='polite'
aria-live='assertive'
aria-live='off'
aria-live='urgent'
questions.explanation: 'assertive' will interrupt whatever the screen reader is currently saying. Use sparingly.

questions.text: What does the 'Focus Visible' success criterion require?
options: That the focus ring is always blue
That any keyboard-focusable element has a visible indicator when it has focus
That the mouse cursor is always visible
That the page zooms in when an item is focused
questions.explanation: Users navigating by keyboard must be able to visually identify which element currently has focus.

questions.text: In the context of accessibility, what is a 'Cognitive Load'?
options: The file size of the JavaScript bundle
The amount of mental effort required to use and understand an interface
The speed at which a user types
The number of images on a page
questions.explanation: Reducing cognitive load involves consistent navigation, clear headings, and avoiding complex, time-sensitive tasks.

questions.text: What is the 'Role' of a custom toggle switch built with a <div> for accessibility?
options: role='button'
role='switch'
role='toggle'
role='checkbox'
questions.explanation: The ARIA role='switch' represents a type of checkbox that represents an on/off state.

questions.text: What is required for a video to be WCAG AA compliant regarding users who are deaf or hard of hearing?
options: A written transcript only
Synchronized captions
Sign language interpretation
Audio descriptions
questions.explanation: Synchronized captions are required for all pre-recorded multimedia with audio.

questions.text: What does 'Info and Relationships' (WCAG 1.3.1) refer to?
options: That the site has a 'Contact Us' page
That information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation are programmatically determinable
That all users are related to each other
That the database is normalized
questions.explanation: This ensures that header-cell relationships in tables, list structures, and form labels are correctly coded for assistive tech.

questions.text: What is a 'Keyboard Trap'?
options: A game played with a keyboard
A situation where a keyboard user can move focus into a component but cannot move focus out of it
A keyboard that is physically broken
A shortcut that closes the browser
questions.explanation: Keyboard traps are common in modals and complex widgets; they are a critical failure in WCAG.

questions.text: How should an error message be associated with a form input programmatically?
options: aria-label
aria-describedby
aria-labelledby
title
questions.explanation: aria-describedby allows the screen reader to announce the error message when the user focus is on the input field.

questions.text: What is the 'Name, Role, Value' success criterion (4.1.2) primarily about?
options: Ensuring the developer's name is in the code
Ensuring that for all user interface components, the name and role can be programmatically determined and state/values can be set
Making sure the website is valuable
Assigning IDs to all elements
questions.explanation: This is the core of accessible custom widgets—ensuring assistive tech knows what an element is and what it's doing.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `inert` attribute for accessibility?
options: To speed up the page
To tell the browser to ignore a sub-tree of the DOM, removing it from the tab order and the accessibility tree
To make an element invisible
To prevent CSS from applying
questions.explanation: Inert is a powerful tool for modals to ensure the background content is completely hidden from assistive tech and keyboard navigation.

questions.text: Which tool is commonly used by Accessibility Specialists for automated testing of WCAG violations?
options: React DevTools
Axe-core / Lighthouse
Postman
Wireshark
questions.explanation: Axe-core is the industry standard engine for automated accessibility testing.

questions.text: What does the 'Status Messages' (WCAG 4.1.3) criterion require?
options: That all messages are in bold
That status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties so they can be presented to the user without focus
That the website status is always 200 OK
That the user is notified when they are offline
questions.explanation: This is often achieved using aria-live regions for things like search results count or success confirmations.

questions.text: True or False: Using 'Color' as the only visual means of conveying information (e.g., error status) is acceptable.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Information must be accessible to users who cannot perceive color; use text labels or icons in addition to color.

questions.text: What is 'Focus Management' in a Single Page Application (SPA)?
options: Managing the user's concentration
Programmatically moving the keyboard focus when a new 'page' is loaded or a modal is opened
Making the search bar larger
Tracking how long a user looks at a button
questions.explanation: In SPAs, focus must be handled manually to prevent users from being 'left behind' when the DOM changes dynamically.

questions.text: Which ARIA attribute indicates that an element is currently expanded or collapsed?
options: aria-expanded
aria-popup
aria-hidden
aria-open
questions.explanation: aria-expanded='true' or 'false' tells assistive tech the state of accordions or menus.

questions.text: What is the requirement for 'Resize Text' in WCAG AA?
options: Text must be resizable up to 50%
Text must be resizable up to 200% without loss of content or functionality
Text must be available in multiple languages
The font must be at least 16px
questions.explanation: Users must be able to zoom the page or increase font size without breaking the layout or hiding features.

questions.text: What is a 'Screen Reader'?
options: A device that cleans the monitor
Assistive technology that renders text and image content as speech or braille output
A person who reads to you
An AI that writes code
questions.explanation: Screen readers like NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver are essential tools for blind or low-vision users.

questions.text: What is the 'Pause, Stop, Hide' requirement for moving content?
options: Animations must stop after 1 minute
Users must be able to control (pause/stop) any moving, blinking, or scrolling information that starts automatically and lasts more than 5 seconds
Moving content is prohibited
Only the admin can stop animations
questions.explanation: This is critical for users with ADHD or seizure disorders to prevent distraction or physical harm.

questions.text: Which HTML element provides the most accessible way to build a tabbed interface?
options: <div> with custom JS
Standard HTML tags with aria-role='tablist', 'tab', and 'tabpanel'
<nav> with <a> tags
<iframe>
questions.explanation: Using specific ARIA roles for tabs ensures screen readers understand the structure and relationship between the triggers and panels.

questions.text: What is 'Consistent Identification' in WCAG?
options: Using the same color for all buttons
Components that have the same functionality across a set of pages must be identified consistently (e.g., icons, labels)
Every user must have a unique ID
The logo must always be in the same place
questions.explanation: Consistency helps users with cognitive disabilities navigate and predict behavior.

questions.text: What does the ARIA attribute `aria-modal='true'` indicate?
options: The element is a pop-up window
The element is a modal dialog, and content outside of it should be ignored by the screen reader
The element is beautiful
The element is only for mobile
questions.explanation: aria-modal helps screen readers 'lock' the user into the dialog, though it often needs to be paired with focus-trapping logic.

questions.text: Which of these is a 'Perceivable' principle in POUR?
options: Provide text alternatives for non-text content
Make all functionality available from a keyboard
Maximize compatibility with current and future user agents
Make text content readable and understandable
questions.explanation: POUR stands for Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust. Text alternatives fall under Perceivable.

questions.text: What is 'Target Size (Minimum)' in WCAG 2.2?
options: The size of the overall website
Pointer inputs must have a target size of at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels
The font size of the header
The number of users targeted
questions.explanation: This new criterion helps users with motor impairments or those using small devices avoid clicking the wrong item.
